family_history_questions.explain_yes_answers_here:
My mom’s younger sister (my maternal aunt, Angela Martinez) has had several episodes of fainting over the last 10–15 years that her doctors have not been able to fully explain. She is currently in her late 40s. The fainting spells usually happen when she stands up quickly or is in a very warm, crowded environment, like on public transportation in the summer or at big family gatherings when she has been on her feet a long time. She has gone to the ER a few times after passing out, and they have checked her heart, blood sugar, and done CT scans, but they have never given her a clear single cause. One cardiologist mentioned that it might be a combination of low blood pressure and vasovagal syncope, but they also said they couldn’t 100% rule out a rhythm issue even though the tests they did at the time were normal. She has never been diagnosed with epilepsy, but at least one of the fainting episodes looked like a brief seizure to family members because she stiffened up and had some shaking for a few seconds while unconscious. She is not on any seizure medications. As far as we know, there is no official diagnosis beyond “unexplained syncope,” and she has just been told to stay hydrated, avoid standing up too fast, and sit or lie down if she feels lightheaded.

On my dad’s side, my paternal grandfather, Miguel Rivera, developed heart rhythm problems (arrhythmias) in his late 60s. He started experiencing episodes of feeling his heart racing or “fluttering” in his chest, sometimes accompanied by mild dizziness and shortness of breath when he was walking up stairs or doing yard work. He was eventually diagnosed with atrial fibrillation and some episodes of bradycardia (his heart beating too slowly). Because of the combination of irregular and sometimes slow heartbeats, and a couple of near-fainting episodes, his cardiologist recommended that he get a pacemaker. He had a dual-chamber pacemaker implanted when he was around 71 years old. Since then, his symptoms have been much better controlled. He goes in for regular follow-ups every 6–12 months to have the device checked, and as far as we know, he has not had any major complications.

He is the only family member we know of who has a pacemaker or implanted defibrillator. No one in the family has been diagnosed with any of the specific genetic heart rhythm syndromes listed (like long QT, Brugada, CPVT, or ARVC), and no one has had a heart attack or sudden unexplained death at a young age. Most relatives who have had heart issues developed them later in life (over age 60) and they were related to high blood pressure, high cholesterol, or age-related changes rather than sudden events.

To summarize the “yes” answers: 
- Unexplained fainting/seizures: maternal aunt Angela, multiple episodes over about a decade, no clear single diagnosis, possibly vasovagal/low blood pressure; one episode looked seizure-like but no confirmed seizure disorder.
- Heart rhythm problems: paternal grandfather Miguel, diagnosed with atrial fibrillation and bradycardia in his late 60s.
- Pacemaker/implanted device: same paternal grandfather Miguel received a dual-chamber pacemaker at around age 71 to manage his arrhythmias and prevent further near-fainting episodes.
